overview.name: ai-native-ds
overview.description: Design system built for AI agents as first-class consumers.
conventions: Pick palette by intent: primary for canonical actions, danger for destructive, neutral for secondary
Disabled state = opacity 40 + pointer-events none, NOT a different palette

COMPONENT CONSUMPTION: components are React components. Import and use them as JSX primitives. Do NOT reimplement components, write raw HTML elements when a component exists, or recreate variant logic in custom CSS. The system's components handle their own variants, hover states, focus rings, and disabled treatment.

exampleOfCorrectUsage.description: A confirmation dialog using only the design system's components
exampleOfCorrectUsage.code:
Delete account?

This action cannot be undone.
exampleOfCorrectUsage.note: Note what is NOT in the code: no custom Button reimplementation, no raw <button> tags, no inline color styles, no hand-rolled hover handlers. The system handles variants, hover, focus rings, and disabled treatment.

sm.$description: Subtle shadow for slightly raised elements

badge.name: Badge
badge.description: A small visual label used to annotate or call out a status, count, or category. For interactive states or filtering, use Tag. For user-generated labels, use Chip.

tone.description: Semantic meaning of the badge, drives palette selection.

guidance.neutral: Default for non-semantic labels (categories, counts, generic metadata).
guidance.success: Positive states: completed, verified, active, paid.
guidance.warning: Caution states: pending, needs review, attention required.
guidance.danger: Critical states: failed, expired, blocked, error.

size.description: Physical size. Use sm in dense contexts like tables or cards.

children.description: Badge label. Keep under 2 words when possible.

requirements: Color alone must not convey meaning — pair with text label
If badge communicates dynamic state changes, wrap in aria-live region in parent

do: Keep labels 1–2 words
Use tone that matches the semantic state (success for completed, not info)
Pair color with text — never rely on color alone
dont: Don't use as interactive button — use Tag instead
Don't use for long text or sentences
Don't combine multiple tones in one group without clear reason

antiPatterns.why: Badge is a passive label, not an action trigger. Clickable semantics require a Button.

use_instead_when.when: label is interactive (filter, removable)
label represents user-generated content

button.name: Button
button.description: Triggers an action. Use for: submitting forms, confirming dialogs, opening menus, primary actions in views. Not for navigation between pages — use Link. Not for binary toggles — use ToggleButton.

palette.description: Semantic intent of the action, drives color treatment.

guidance.neutral: Default for most buttons. Use for: cancel, back, secondary actions, tertiary actions. The bulk of buttons in any UI should be neutral.
guidance.primary: The single most important action in a view. Use for: submit, save, continue, confirm. Only one primary button per view, ever.
guidance.danger: Irreversible or destructive actions. Use for: delete, remove, cancel-subscription, reset. Pair with confirmation when consequence is severe.

variant.description: Visual treatment. Determines which palette slots are consumed.

guidance.solid: Filled background using palette base color. Highest visual weight. Reserve for primary and destructive actions where emphasis matters.
guidance.subtle: Soft tinted background. Medium visual weight. Default for neutral palette buttons. Use when the action is important but shouldn't dominate.
guidance.ghost: Transparent background, palette-tinted text. Lowest visual weight. Use for tertiary actions, toolbar buttons, dense UIs.

size.description: Physical size. sm in dense contexts (toolbars, table rows). md is default. lg for hero CTAs or mobile primary actions.

loading.description: Replaces button content with a spinner. Disables interaction while loading. Preserves button width to prevent layout shift.

disabled.description: Prevents interaction. Reduces opacity to indicate non-interactive state. Disabled appearance is palette-agnostic — neutral, primary, and danger all dim the same way.

iconStart.description: Icon rendered before the label. Pair with text, not as standalone (use IconButton for icon-only).

iconEnd.description: Icon rendered after the label. Common for: chevrons indicating dropdown, arrows for next/forward.

type.description: Native button type attribute. Use 'submit' inside forms. Default 'button' prevents accidental form submission.

children.description: Button label. Use a verb describing the action: 'Save changes', 'Delete account'. Avoid 'OK', 'Submit' without context.

requirements: Must have accessible label via children or aria-label
Loading state must announce via aria-busy='true'
Disabled state uses aria-disabled (not the disabled attribute) when context requires the button stay focusable for screen reader navigation
Focus ring must be visible — never set outline:none without replacement
Icon-only buttons require aria-label (or use IconButton component)
accessibility.contrast: 4.5:1 minimum for text. Solid style buttons meet this via palette/contrast pairing. When disabled, the 0.4 opacity reduces effective contrast — this is intentional and signals non-interactivity.

do: Use verbs that describe the action: 'Save changes', 'Delete account', 'Send invite'
One primary button per view — the single most important action
Pair destructive buttons with confirmation when consequences are severe
Place primary action on the right in LTR dialog footers
Use type='submit' inside forms; type='button' everywhere else
dont: Don't use 'Click here', 'OK', or 'Submit' without context
Don't use multiple primary buttons in the same view
Don't use a button for navigation — use Link
Don't override the disabled opacity — the dim is the affordance
Don't put icons on both sides unless one is decorative (chevron) and one is meaningful

examples.name: Destructive with confirmation
examples.context: Settings page, paired with confirmation dialog

antiPatterns.why: Cancel is a low-emphasis action. Solid primary reserved for the canonical action of the view.
Navigation belongs to Link. Buttons trigger actions. Destructive palette implies destructive action.
Disabled appearance is the visual cue users expect. Overriding opacity keeps the button looking active when it isn't, which is misleading.

use_instead_when.when: navigating to another page or view
icon-only button, no text label
binary on/off state
trigger for a menu — has its own composition

card.name: Card
card.description: Container for grouped content. Composes with Card.Header, Card.Body, Card.Footer subcomponents to create structured layouts. Card is structurally neutral — the content within carries intent (use Badge, callout patterns, etc. for emphasis). The variant prop controls visual elevation; the padding prop sets uniform rhythm across all subcomponents.

guidance.flat: No border, no shadow. Use for cards within cards, or sections that need grouping but not visual separation.
guidance.outlined: Subtle border, no shadow. The default — works in most contexts.
guidance.raised: Border + shadow. Use when the card needs to feel above the surrounding content (hover-to-elevate patterns, floating action panels, summary cards on a feed).

padding.description: Uniform padding scale applied to Card.Header, Card.Body, and Card.Footer.

guidance.sm: Compact. Good for cards in dense lists or sidebars.
guidance.md: Default. Comfortable for most form/content contexts.
guidance.lg: Generous. Hero cards, marketing surfaces, primary feature cards.

as.description: Render as a different element (e.g. 'article', 'section', 'aside'). Useful for semantic HTML — a card representing a blog post should be 'article', a card representing related content should be 'aside'.

requirements: Use the 'as' prop to provide semantic meaning where appropriate (article, section, aside) — Card defaults to div which has no implicit semantics
If the entire card is interactive (clickable card), wrap a single Card in a link or button rather than putting interactive elements inside that compete for the click target
Card.Header should typically contain a heading element — use h2/h3/h4 inside Card.Header based on the page's heading hierarchy
Card itself does not get role='region' by default — apply only when the card is a discrete navigational landmark

composition.subcomponentOrdering: Card.Header → Card.Body → Card.Footer is the canonical order. Body alone is acceptable. Skipping Body in favor of just Header + Footer is unusual and probably indicates the wrong component.

subcomponents.name: Card.Header
subcomponents.description: Top section of the card. Typically contains a heading, optionally with metadata or accessory content (badges, icons, dropdowns). Inherits Card's padding scale.

children.description: Heading element and any accessory content. Recommended: a heading (h2/h3/h4) plus optional Badge or icon.

do: Put a heading element here as the primary child
Combine heading with accessory elements (Badge, status indicator, dropdown trigger) using flex layout
Keep header content visually consistent — avoid mixing text-heavy descriptions here, those belong in Body
dont: Don't put long descriptions or paragraphs here — Body is for that
Don't put primary actions here — Footer is the conventional place for actions
Don't render Card.Header as the only child of Card — that's a Card without content; consider whether you actually need a card

examples.name: Heading with badge
examples.context: Project summary card on a dashboard

subcomponents.name: Card.Body
subcomponents.description: Primary content area. Holds the bulk of the card's information — descriptions, lists, embedded content, form fields. Inherits Card's padding scale.

children.description: Primary content. Can be any combination of text, form elements, lists, or embedded components.

do: Put the main content here — descriptions, data, form fields
Use semantic HTML inside (paragraphs, lists, sections) rather than nested divs
Body is the only required subcomponent if you're using subcomponents at all
dont: Don't repeat heading content here that belongs in Card.Header
Don't put primary actions here — those go in Footer
Don't introduce custom padding — Card's padding prop controls this

examples.name: Text content
examples.code: Last deploy: 2 hours ago. Three pending changes.

subcomponents.name: Card.Footer
subcomponents.description: Bottom section. Holds actions (buttons), metadata (timestamps, attribution), or both. Inherits Card's padding scale. Often visually separated from Body via a top border.

children.description: Actions, metadata, or both. Most commonly a row of buttons.

do: Use Footer for primary card actions (buttons)
Use Footer for low-emphasis metadata (timestamps, author bylines, status info)
When combining actions and metadata, layout them with metadata on the left and actions on the right (LTR)
If multiple buttons, follow the Button spec's primary-action-on-the-right rule
dont: Don't put more than one primary action in Footer — competing primaries violate the system's one-primary-per-view rule
Don't put descriptive content here — that belongs in Body
Don't omit Card.Footer just to save space — if there are actions, the Footer is the right place for them

examples.name: Metadata + action
examples.code: Updated 2 hours ago

do: Use Card to group related content that belongs together
Match variant to context: outlined for in-flow content, raised for floating/elevated content, flat for nested cards
Use semantic HTML via 'as' prop when the card represents a meaningful document part (article, aside, section)
Use subcomponents (Header/Body/Footer) for structured cards; raw children are fine for simple cards
Match Card padding to surrounding context: sm in dense lists, md as default, lg for hero or marketing cards
dont: Don't add palette tinting to Card itself — Card is structurally neutral. If you need to indicate intent, put a Badge or callout inside Card.Body.
Don't nest raised cards inside raised cards — the elevation hierarchy collapses visually. Use flat for inner cards.
Don't customize per-subcomponent padding — the uniform rhythm is intentional. Use sm/md/lg on Card itself.
Don't use Card as a generic styling utility — if you just need a styled box, that's a div. Card implies grouped content with semantic meaning.

examples.name: Simple outlined card
examples.context: Basic card on a content page

examples.name: Card with footer actions
examples.code:
Confirm changes

Review before saving.

examples.name: Raised hero card
examples.code:
Welcome back

Here's what's new since your last visit.

examples.name: Compact card in a list
examples.code:
Item title

New

Brief description.
examples.context: Item in a feed or search results

examples.name: Card without subcomponents
examples.context: Lightweight grouped content where structure isn't needed

antiPatterns.why: Nested raised cards collapse the elevation hierarchy — the inner shadow disappears against the outer shadow. Inner cards should be flat or outlined to preserve the parent's elevation.
Card is structurally neutral. To convey intent, put intent-bearing content (Badge, callouts) inside the card. Tinting the card itself is the wrong abstraction layer.
Two primary actions in the same Footer violate the system's one-primary-action-per-view rule. The user shouldn't have to decide which solid primary button to click.

Per-subcomponent padding overrides break Card's intentional uniform rhythm. If you need less padding, set Card's padding prop to 'sm'. If you need wildly different spacing across subcomponents, you may not actually want a Card.

use_instead_when.when: the content needs to be modal or focus-trapping
you just need a styled box without grouped semantic meaning
you need a major page region; Card is for smaller groupings within a region

checkbox.name: Checkbox
checkbox.description: Binary or tri-state checkbox built on native input[type=checkbox]. Composes with FormField for label, helper, and error layout. Supports an indeterminate state for 'some but not all' selections (e.g., a select-all checkbox when only some children are checked).

size.description: Physical size of the checkbox. sm for dense lists, md default, lg for touch-primary or emphasis.

checked.description: Controlled checked state. If provided, Checkbox is controlled.

indeterminate.description: Tri-state indicator for 'some but not all'. Visually distinct from both checked and unchecked. Common for a parent checkbox controlling a group. Note: indeterminate is a visual/ARIA state — the underlying input value is still either checked or unchecked.

disabled.description: Prevents interaction. Reduces opacity to indicate non-interactive state.

name.description: Form field name for data submission.

value.description: Value submitted when checked, if part of a form.

requirements: Must have an accessible label — via FormField.Label, aria-label, or aria-labelledby
Indeterminate state must set aria-checked='mixed' (not just the visual treatment)
Focus ring must be visible
Disabled checkboxes should still be readable by screen readers, just not interactive

do: Wrap in FormField for label and layout
Use indeterminate for parent checkboxes controlling a group
Use Checkbox for independent binary choices — if choices are mutually exclusive, use Radio
dont: Don't use Checkbox for mutually exclusive choices — that's Radio's job
Don't override the disabled opacity
Don't use indeterminate as a permanent state — it represents a transitional 'some selected' condition

antiPatterns.why: A standalone span next to a checkbox has no programmatic association. FormField wires the label correctly.

use_instead_when.when: choices are mutually exclusive
the choice toggles a setting on/off with immediate effect

dialog.name: Dialog
dialog.description: Modal dialog. Built on the native HTML <dialog> element with showModal() semantics: focus trap, escape-to-close, and top-layer rendering handled by the browser. Composes with Dialog.Title (required for accessibility), Dialog.Description (optional but recommended), Dialog.Body, Dialog.Footer, and Dialog.Trigger. For non-modal floating content, use Popover (separate component, future).

guidance.sm: 400px max-width. Use for confirmations, simple prompts, login forms.
guidance.md: 560px max-width. Default. Comfortable for most forms, settings panels, content review.
guidance.lg: 720px max-width. Use for complex forms, multi-step flows, side-by-side comparisons. Beyond this size, consider a dedicated page instead of a dialog.

open.description: Controlled open state. When provided, Dialog is controlled — parent owns the open/closed state. When omitted, Dialog manages its own state via Dialog.Trigger.

onOpenChange.description: Called when the dialog requests state change (trigger click, escape key, backdrop click). Required if `open` is provided.

closeOnBackdropClick.description: Whether clicking the backdrop closes the dialog. Set to false for dialogs where accidental dismissal would lose user work (multi-step forms, destructive confirmations).

closeOnEscape.description: Whether the Escape key closes the dialog. Almost always true. Set to false only for dialogs that explicitly require an action (e.g., a 'must agree to terms' gate).

initialFocusRef.description: Element to focus when the dialog opens. Defaults to the first focusable element in the dialog content. Use this to focus a specific input, the destructive button in a destructive dialog (so escape is the easier path), or a primary action.

requirements: Must have Dialog.Title — maps to aria-labelledby on the dialog element. Without it, screen readers announce the dialog with no name.
Dialog.Description recommended for any dialog where the action or content needs explanation. Maps to aria-describedby.
Focus trap is handled by the native <dialog> showModal() behavior — Tab and Shift+Tab cycle within the dialog only.
On open: focus moves to the dialog (specifically: initialFocusRef if provided, otherwise the first focusable element, otherwise the dialog itself).
On close: focus returns to the element that opened the dialog (typically the Dialog.Trigger).
Escape closes the dialog by default. Override only with strong justification (e.g., must-acknowledge gate).
The backdrop is rendered by the browser's ::backdrop pseudo-element and is non-interactive in modal mode.
Multiple dialogs at once are unsupported in v0.1 — open a new dialog requires closing the current one. Nested modal dialogs are a known UX antipattern.
accessibility.contrast: Dialog content must meet 4.5:1 minimum on the overlay background. The overlay background is color.common.surface.overlay which is slightly elevated from surface.base — verify contrast on whatever palette consumers put inside the dialog.

composition.subcomponentOrdering: Dialog.Trigger first (outside the dialog content), then Dialog.Title → Description → Body → Footer. Title is required. Trigger is optional if Dialog is controlled.

subcomponents.name: Dialog.Trigger
subcomponents.description: Element that opens the dialog. Typically wraps a Button. The trigger element receives focus when the dialog closes (focus restoration).

children.description: The trigger element. Most commonly a Button.

asChild.description: When true, Dialog.Trigger renders its child directly (passes props through) instead of wrapping in its own element. Use this when wrapping a Button or other interactive element to avoid nested buttons.

do: Use asChild=true when wrapping a Button to prevent nested <button> elements
Place Dialog.Trigger as the first child of Dialog so it's outside the dialog content
The trigger should describe what the dialog will do ('Delete account', 'Edit settings'), not just label its existence ('Open dialog')
dont: Don't put Dialog.Trigger inside Dialog.Body or Dialog.Footer — it's a sibling of those, not a child
Don't use asChild=true with non-interactive elements like <div> — the trigger needs to be a real button for keyboard accessibility
Don't render multiple Dialog.Trigger elements in one Dialog — if you need multiple triggers, use controlled mode (open/onOpenChange) with separate buttons

examples.name: Button trigger
examples.code:
<Dialog>
  <Dialog.Trigger asChild>
    <Button palette="danger" variant="solid">Delete account</Button>
  </Dialog.Trigger>
  <Dialog.Title>Delete your account?</Dialog.Title>
  ...
</Dialog>

subcomponents.name: Dialog.Title
subcomponents.description: Required heading for the dialog. Maps to aria-labelledby. Rendered as an h2 by default.

children.description: The title text. Keep it short and action-oriented.

as.description: HTML element to render as. Default 'h2'. Use 'h1' only if the dialog is the primary content of the page (rare).

do: Phrase the title as a question for confirmations ('Delete your account?')
Phrase the title as a statement for informational dialogs ('Account deleted')
Keep titles under ~50 characters — they're focusing elements, not full sentences
dont: Don't omit Dialog.Title — even decorative dialogs need an accessible name
Don't put long form labels in the title — those belong in Dialog.Body or in the form itself
Don't use 'Dialog' or 'Modal' as the title — describe what the dialog does

subcomponents.name: Dialog.Description
subcomponents.description: Optional descriptive text below the title. Maps to aria-describedby. Provides context, consequences, or instructions related to the dialog's action.

children.description: Description text. Typically one or two sentences.

do: Use Dialog.Description for consequences of destructive actions ('This cannot be undone')
Use Dialog.Description for context that helps the user decide ('Your collaborators will lose access')
Keep descriptions concise — long explanations belong in Dialog.Body
dont: Don't repeat the title in the description
Don't put form fields or interactive content in Description — that's Body's job
Don't omit Description for destructive actions — the consequence statement is what makes the confirmation meaningful

examples.name: Destructive consequence
examples.code: <Dialog.Description>This action is permanent and cannot be undone. Your projects, files, and collaborator access will be removed.</Dialog.Description>

subcomponents.name: Dialog.Body
subcomponents.description: Main content area. Use for form fields, lists, embedded content, or anything that doesn't fit the title/description/footer pattern.

children.description: Body content. Can include form fields, paragraphs, lists, or embedded components.

do: Use Body for form content in dialogs with input (Input, Select, Checkbox, etc.)
Use Body for content that's longer than Description allows
Body is optional — simple confirmation dialogs may only need Title + Description + Footer
dont: Don't put the title or description here — they have their own subcomponents
Don't put primary actions here — those belong in Dialog.Footer
Don't make Body extremely tall — if scrolling is required, consider a dedicated page instead of a dialog

subcomponents.name: Dialog.Footer
subcomponents.description: Footer with action buttons. Buttons are end-aligned (LTR) with primary action on the right. Top border separator.

children.description: Action buttons. Typically a Cancel + primary action pair.

do: Place the primary action on the right (LTR): Cancel + Confirm, Cancel + Save, Cancel + Delete
Use one primary action per dialog — competing primaries force the user to choose between two solid buttons
Use palette='danger' variant='solid' for destructive primary actions, palette='primary' variant='solid' for constructive
Use palette='neutral' variant='subtle' for the Cancel action
dont: Don't reverse the order (Confirm + Cancel) — LTR convention is Cancel on left, primary on right
Don't include three or more buttons — if there are three actions, the dialog is doing too much
Don't omit a way to dismiss — even a button-less dialog needs Escape and backdrop close enabled

do: Always include Dialog.Title — accessibility requires it
Use Dialog.Description for any non-trivial action, especially destructive ones
Set closeOnBackdropClick={false} for destructive dialogs and multi-step forms where accidental dismissal loses work
Use initialFocusRef to focus the Cancel button (not the destructive action) in destructive dialogs — makes Escape the path of least resistance
Keep dialog content focused on a single task — if you find yourself adding tabs or stepping through multi-page content, consider a dedicated page
Use size='sm' for simple confirmations, 'md' for forms, 'lg' for complex content
dont: Don't nest dialogs — open a second dialog requires closing the first. Native <dialog> behavior enforces this
Don't disable Escape key closing without strong justification — Escape is a fundamental keyboard expectation
Don't override the focus trap — the native <dialog> handles it correctly
Don't put navigation links or non-action buttons in Dialog.Footer — Footer is for the dialog's primary actions
Don't use Dialog for non-modal content (toasts, popovers, drawers) — those have their own components or are deferred

examples.name: Destructive confirmation
examples.code:
<Dialog>
  <Dialog.Trigger asChild>
    <Button palette="danger" variant="solid">Delete account</Button>
  </Dialog.Trigger>
  <Dialog.Title>Delete your account?</Dialog.Title>
  <Dialog.Description>This action is permanent and cannot be undone. Your projects, files, and collaborator access will be removed.</Dialog.Description>
  <Dialog.Footer>
    <Button palette="neutral" variant="subtle">Cancel</Button>
    <Button palette="danger" variant="solid">Delete account</Button>
  </Dialog.Footer>
</Dialog>

examples.name: Form dialog
examples.code:
<Dialog size="md" closeOnBackdropClick={false}>
  <Dialog.Trigger asChild>
    <Button palette="primary" variant="solid">Edit profile</Button>
  </Dialog.Trigger>
  <Dialog.Title>Edit your profile</Dialog.Title>
  <Dialog.Body>
    <FormField label="Name"><Input /></FormField>
    <FormField label="Bio"><Input /></FormField>
  </Dialog.Body>
  <Dialog.Footer>
    <Button palette="neutral" variant="subtle">Cancel</Button>
    <Button palette="primary" variant="solid">Save changes</Button>
  </Dialog.Footer>
</Dialog>

examples.name: Informational acknowledgment
examples.code:
<Dialog size="sm">
  <Dialog.Title>Update available</Dialog.Title>
  <Dialog.Description>A new version of the app is available. The page will reload after you confirm.</Dialog.Description>
  <Dialog.Footer>
    <Button palette="primary" variant="solid">Reload</Button>
  </Dialog.Footer>
</Dialog>

examples.name: Controlled dialog with programmatic open
examples.code:
const [open, setOpen] = useState(false)

<Dialog open={open} onOpenChange={setOpen}>
  <Dialog.Title>Confirm action</Dialog.Title>
  <Dialog.Description>Are you sure?</Dialog.Description>
  <Dialog.Footer>
    <Button palette="neutral" variant="subtle" onClick={() => setOpen(false)}>Cancel</Button>
    <Button palette="primary" variant="solid" onClick={handleConfirm}>Confirm</Button>
  </Dialog.Footer>
</Dialog>

antiPatterns.why: Dialog requires Dialog.Title for accessibility. Without it, screen readers announce a dialog with no name. The descriptive text belongs in Dialog.Description, not Dialog.Body.
LTR convention is Cancel on the left, primary action on the right. Reversing the order breaks the user's muscle memory and increases the risk of accidental clicks.

Disabling both Escape and backdrop click creates a dialog with no dismissal affordance other than action buttons. This is a known accessibility antipattern unless the dialog represents a mandatory gate (terms of service, password requirement). For informational dialogs, leave the defaults.

antiPatterns.good:
<Dialog>
  <Dialog.Trigger asChild>
    <Button>Edit</Button>
  </Dialog.Trigger>
  <Dialog.Title>Edit settings</Dialog.Title>
  <Dialog.Body>...form fields...</Dialog.Body>
  <Dialog.Footer>
    <Button palette="neutral" variant="subtle">Cancel</Button>
    <Button palette="primary" variant="solid">Save</Button>
  </Dialog.Footer>
</Dialog>
antiPatterns.why: Primary action buttons belong in Dialog.Footer, not Dialog.Body. Footer is the conventional location for actions, has correct visual treatment (top border, end-aligned), and signals to the user where to look for what to do next.

use_instead_when.when: the content is non-modal (doesn't block underlying interaction) — future component
the content is a transient notification — future component
the content slides in from an edge (drawer pattern) — future component
the content is complex enough that scrolling or multi-step navigation is needed

form-field.name: FormField
form-field.description: Layout wrapper for form controls. Provides label, helper text, error message, and required indicator with correct ARIA wiring. Auto-generates ID and htmlFor association via React Context so consumers don't have to manage them manually. Compose with Input, Checkbox, Radio, Switch, Select, or any form control as its single inner control child.

required.description: Marks the field as required. Renders a visual indicator next to the label. Does not enforce validation — that's the inner control's job. Use for visual signaling that the field must be filled.

invalid.description: Marks the field as having a validation error. Consumers typically set this when FormField.Error is also being rendered. Used to propagate state to the inner control (e.g., Input renders with state='error' when its FormField is invalid).

id.description: Override the auto-generated ID. Use this when the field needs to be referenced from elsewhere (e.g., from an aria-controls relationship on another component). If omitted, FormField generates a unique ID via useId().

requirements: FormField.Label must be associated with the inner control via htmlFor/id. Auto-wired via context.
FormField.Error should be announced when it appears — uses aria-live='polite' and is referenced by aria-describedby on the inner control.
FormField.Helper is non-critical context, referenced by aria-describedby on the inner control.
The required indicator is visual only — for screen reader announcement, the inner control must also have aria-required='true' (typically through its native required attribute).
The invalid prop maps to aria-invalid='true' on the inner control via context.

allowedChildren: any form control (Input, Checkbox, Radio, Switch, Select)
composition.subcomponentOrdering: FormField.Label first, then the control, then FormField.Helper or FormField.Error (or both). Label is required for accessibility. Helper and Error are optional but at least one is typical for form fields.

subcomponents.name: FormField.Label
subcomponents.description: Label for the form field. Automatically wires htmlFor to the inner control's ID. Renders the required indicator if FormField has required=true.

do: Phrase labels as nouns or short noun phrases: 'Email', 'Full name', 'Date of birth'
Place FormField.Label as the first child of FormField
Use sentence case unless system convention requires otherwise
dont: Don't include required indicator manually — FormField's required prop handles this
Don't include 'optional' as part of the label text — optional should be the default; mark required fields instead
Don't put long descriptions in the label — those belong in FormField.Helper

subcomponents.name: FormField.Helper
subcomponents.description: Helper text below the control. Provides context, format hints, or examples. Non-critical information that helps the user complete the field correctly.

children.description: Helper content. Typically one short sentence.

do: Use helper text for format examples ('e.g., name@company.com'), constraints ('Minimum 8 characters'), or context ('Visible to your team only')
Keep it short — one sentence
Render helper text below the control, above any error message
dont: Don't put error messages here — use FormField.Error
Don't repeat the label content
Don't render Helper and Error simultaneously for the same condition — Error replaces Helper when both are present

examples.name: Format hint
examples.code: <FormField.Helper>Use a strong password with at least 12 characters.</FormField.Helper>

subcomponents.name: FormField.Error
subcomponents.description: Error message below the control. Replaces helper text when present. Marked with aria-live so screen readers announce the error when it appears.

children.description: Error message. Specific and actionable — describe what's wrong and how to fix it.

do: Be specific: 'Email must include @' is better than 'Invalid email'
Suggest a fix when possible: 'Password too short — minimum 8 characters'
Render Error only when there's a real validation error — empty FormField.Error elements are noise
Pair FormField.Error with FormField invalid=true so the inner control also gets the error state
dont: Don't render generic errors like 'Required' — be specific about what's required and why
Don't render Error and Helper simultaneously — Error replaces Helper
Don't include the field name in the error if the label already shows it — 'Required' is redundant when the Email label already shows the field

do: Wrap all form controls in FormField for consistent label/helper/error layout
Use FormField.Label as the first child — accessibility requires it
Set required=true and pair with FormField.Error when needed
Pair invalid=true with FormField.Error so the inner control's state reflects the validation
Let FormField auto-generate IDs — manual ID management is error-prone
dont: Don't render form controls without FormField unless the form is decorative or temporary
Don't manually set id/htmlFor — FormField handles this via context
Don't nest FormField inside FormField
Don't include label text inside the form control (e.g., Input's placeholder is not a label) — use FormField.Label
Don't render multiple form controls inside one FormField — one field per FormField

examples.name: With helper text
examples.code:
<FormField>
  <FormField.Label>Password</FormField.Label>
  <Input type="password" />
  <FormField.Helper>Minimum 8 characters with at least one number.</FormField.Helper>
</FormField>

examples.name: Required field with error
examples.code:
<FormField required invalid>
  <FormField.Label>Full name</FormField.Label>
  <Input value="" />
  <FormField.Error>Name is required.</FormField.Error>
</FormField>
examples.context: Form submitted with empty required field

examples.name: With Checkbox
examples.code:
<FormField>
  <FormField.Label>Subscribe to newsletter</FormField.Label>
  <Checkbox />
  <FormField.Helper>You can unsubscribe at any time.</FormField.Helper>
</FormField>
examples.context: Boolean opt-in (Checkbox composes the same way as Input)

antiPatterns.why: Manual ID management is error-prone (forget one, screen readers silently fail). The * indicator for required should come from FormField's required prop, not manually appended to label text. Both are concerns FormField handles automatically.
Placeholders are not labels. They disappear when typing, fail screen reader requirements, and offer no accessible name. FormField provides the label correctly.
'Required' in helper text duplicates what the required prop already signals visually. Use FormField's required prop and reserve Helper for actually-helpful information.
FormField wraps one control. Two controls in one FormField breaks the label association (the label can only point to one ID) and confuses screen readers about what the field is.

use_instead_when.when: you need to group multiple related fields under one legend — not yet built
wrapping a set of Radio controls that share semantics — handled internally by Radio component

input.name: Input
input.description: Single-line text input. Composes with FormField for label/helper/error layout, and with Input.LeadingIcon / Input.TrailingIcon for adornments. Type-agnostic at this version: text only. Future: email, password, number, tel, etc.

state.description: Validation state. Distinct from Button's palette — this is intrinsic to the input's current value, not the action's intent.

guidance.default: Initial state. No validation has fired or all fields are valid.
guidance.error: Field has a validation problem. Pair with a FormField error message.
guidance.success: Field has passed validation. Use sparingly — every valid field doesn't need a green ring.

guidance.outlined: Default. Border on all sides, transparent background. Works on any surface.
guidance.filled: Tinted background, no visible border at rest. Good for compact forms or when reducing visual noise.
guidance.ghost: Transparent background, border only at bottom. Use sparingly — search bars, inline editing, dense UIs.

size.description: Physical size. sm in dense contexts (table rows, toolbars). md is default. lg for hero forms or mobile-primary inputs.

disabled.description: Prevents interaction. Reduces opacity to indicate non-interactive state. Disabled appearance is state-agnostic — disabled error and disabled success look the same as disabled default.

readonly.description: Prevents editing but keeps the value selectable and copyable. Different from disabled: readonly fields are still interactive for reading.

placeholder.description: Placeholder text shown when value is empty. Not a substitute for a label — placeholders disappear when typing and have known a11y problems.

value.description: Controlled value. If provided, Input is controlled; otherwise it manages its own state.

name.description: Form field name. Required if used inside a form for data submission.

id.description: DOM id. Important when paired with FormField — the FormField label's htmlFor must match this id.

effects.transition: border-color 120ms ease, background-color 120ms ease, opacity 120ms ease

requirements: Must have an accessible label — either via FormField's <label htmlFor>, aria-label, or aria-labelledby
Error state must be announced — pair with FormField error message that has aria-live='polite' or aria-describedby
Focus ring must be visible — never set outline:none without replacement
Placeholder is NOT a label substitute — placeholders fail screen reader tests and disappear when typing
accessibility.contrast: 4.5:1 minimum for input text. State borders (error/success) must be visible without relying on color alone — pair with iconography or text in FormField.

subcomponents.name: Input.LeadingIcon
subcomponents.description: Icon rendered inside the input on the left (LTR). Inherits the input's text color and adjusts padding so input text doesn't overlap the icon.

do: Use for visual signals like a search magnifier, calendar icon for date input, or @ for email
Keep icons monochromatic — they inherit currentColor
dont: Don't use as an interactive button — leading icon is a visual cue, not a control. Use Input.TrailingIcon for interactive elements like clear-buttons.
Don't put text in here. Icons only.

subcomponents.name: Input.TrailingIcon
subcomponents.description: Icon rendered inside the input on the right (LTR). Can be visual (validation indicator) or interactive (clear button, password toggle).

children.description: Icon element or button containing icon.

interactive.description: Whether the icon is clickable. When true, increases hit target and changes cursor.

do: Use interactive=true for clear buttons, password visibility toggles, dropdown triggers
When interactive, the child should be a Button or button-shaped element with proper aria-label
dont: Don't put a Button component inside without interactive=true — clicks won't have proper hit area
Don't combine validation icons with interactive icons in the same trailing slot — split into a separate adornment if needed

do: Pair with FormField for label, helper text, and error messages
Use placeholder for examples or format hints, not as a label substitute
Match input size to surrounding form: sm for compact tables, md for forms, lg for hero auth pages
When using state='error', also surface the error message in the FormField's error slot
Set name and id when used in real forms — both are needed for label association and form submission
dont: Don't use placeholder as a label — known a11y failure pattern
Don't use state='success' for every valid field — green rings on every input is visual noise
Don't override the disabled opacity — the dim is the affordance
Don't put a Button as a direct child — use Input.TrailingIcon with interactive=true
Don't reach for ghost variant outside of search bars or inline editing — outlined is the default for a reason

examples.name: Error state
examples.context: Form field with validation error — pair with FormField error message

examples.name: Disabled input
examples.context: Account email that can't be changed

antiPatterns.why: Placeholders disappear when typing and have known accessibility problems. Use FormField for the label, placeholder for format hints.
Error visual without an error message means the user knows something is wrong but not what. Always pair state='error' with explanatory text in FormField.
Button as a direct child won't render with proper hit area or styling inside an input. Use Input.TrailingIcon to compose interactive trailing elements correctly.
Ghost variant has minimal visual affordance and is hard to discover. Reserve for search bars and inline editing where context already signals it's an input. Outlined is the right default for forms.

use_instead_when.when: multi-line text input is needed
choosing from a known list of options
filtering a list with text input
wrapping Input with label, helper, or error message

radio.name: Radio
radio.description: Single option within a mutually-exclusive group. Built on native input[type=radio]. Must be used inside a RadioGroup — RadioGroup owns the shared name, selected value, and change handler. A Radio outside a RadioGroup has no group semantics.

value.description: The value this radio represents. RadioGroup compares this against its selected value to determine checked state. Required.

size.description: Physical size. Usually inherited from RadioGroup; set individually only for unusual layouts.

disabled.description: Disables this specific radio option. The rest of the group remains interactive.

keyboard: Arrow keys move between options in the group
requirements: Radio must be inside a RadioGroup for correct keyboard navigation and group semantics
Each Radio needs an accessible label — via FormField.Label or aria-label
Only one Radio in a group can be selected at a time — enforced by RadioGroup
Arrow key navigation moves selection within the group (native radio behavior)

do: Always use Radio inside a RadioGroup
Give each Radio a unique value within its group
Use Radio when exactly one choice must be selected from a set
dont: Don't use Radio outside a RadioGroup — it loses group semantics and keyboard navigation
Don't use Radio for independent choices — that's Checkbox
Don't use Radio for on/off toggles — that's Switch

antiPatterns.why: Radios outside a RadioGroup have no shared name, no enforced single-selection, and no arrow-key navigation. RadioGroup provides all three.

use_instead_when.when: choices are independent (multiple can be selected)
there are many options and space is limited

radio-group.name: RadioGroup
radio-group.description: Container for a set of mutually-exclusive Radio options. Owns the shared name, the selected value, and the change handler. Provides these to child Radio components via context. Radios inside a RadioGroup automatically get correct single-selection behavior and keyboard navigation.

name.description: Shared form field name applied to all Radios in the group. Required for native radio behavior and form submission.

value.description: Controlled selected value. The Radio whose value matches is rendered as selected.

onChange.description: Called when selection changes. Receives the newly-selected Radio's value.

size.description: Size applied to all Radios in the group. Individual Radios inherit this unless they override.

disabled.description: Disables the entire group. Individual Radios can also be disabled independently.

orientation.description: Layout direction of the radio options. Vertical is default and usually correct; horizontal for short option sets where space allows.

keyboard: Arrow keys move selection between Radios
Tab moves focus into/out of the group as a unit
requirements: RadioGroup has role='radiogroup'
The group should have an accessible label — wrap the RadioGroup in a FormField, or use aria-label/aria-labelledby
Arrow key navigation cycles selection within the group
Tab enters the group at the selected radio (or first radio if none selected) and exits the group entirely on next Tab

do: Always wrap Radio components in a RadioGroup
Give RadioGroup a meaningful name for form submission
Wrap the whole RadioGroup in a FormField if the group needs a label (it usually does)
Use horizontal orientation only for 2-3 short options
dont: Don't put non-Radio interactive content inside RadioGroup
Don't nest RadioGroups
Don't use a RadioGroup with a single Radio — if there's only one option, it's a Checkbox

examples.name: Controlled RadioGroup with FormField label
examples.code:
<FormField>
  <FormField.Label>Subscription plan</FormField.Label>
  <RadioGroup name="plan" value={plan} onChange={setPlan}>
    <FormField><FormField.Label>Free</FormField.Label><Radio value="free" /></FormField>
    <FormField><FormField.Label>Pro</FormField.Label><Radio value="pro" /></FormField>
    <FormField><FormField.Label>Enterprise</FormField.Label><Radio value="enterprise" /></FormField>
  </RadioGroup>
</FormField>
examples.context: Plan picker in a settings form

examples.name: Horizontal orientation
examples.code:
<RadioGroup name="size" defaultValue="md" orientation="horizontal">
  <FormField><FormField.Label>S</FormField.Label><Radio value="sm" /></FormField>
  <FormField><FormField.Label>M</FormField.Label><Radio value="md" /></FormField>
  <FormField><FormField.Label>L</FormField.Label><Radio value="lg" /></FormField>
</RadioGroup>

antiPatterns.why: A RadioGroup with a single option is a binary choice — that's a Checkbox. RadioGroup is for selecting one of several.

use_instead_when.when: there are many options (roughly 6+) or vertical space is constrained
multiple selections should be allowed

spinner.name: Spinner
spinner.description: Indicates ongoing background activity. Inherits color from parent via currentColor — meant to be embedded in components like Button (loading state) or used standalone for fetch indicators. For full-page or section loading, use Skeleton instead.

size.description: Diameter. Match to context: sm inside dense buttons, md for inline indicators, lg for standalone loading states.

label.description: Accessible label announced to screen readers via aria-label. Override when context demands specificity ('Saving changes', 'Sending').

requirements: Must have aria-label describing what is loading
When inside a parent component (e.g., Button), parent should set aria-busy='true' instead of relying on Spinner's role
accessibility.contrast: Inherits parent color, which determines contrast

do: Use inside components that have their own busy semantics (Button) — let parent communicate state
Match size to context (sm in buttons, md inline, lg standalone)
Provide a meaningful aria-label when used standalone
dont: Don't use for content that takes <300ms — flickers and hurts perceived performance
Don't use for full-page loading — Skeleton communicates layout better
Don't override color directly — set color on parent, Spinner inherits

examples.name: Inside Button
examples.context: Button manages spinner via loading prop

examples.name: Standalone inline
examples.context: Inline indicator next to a section title

antiPatterns.why: Spinner inherits via currentColor. Set color on the parent for consistency with the design system.

use_instead_when.when: loading content with known layout (lists, cards)
you can communicate determinate progress

switch.name: Switch
switch.description: Toggle for a setting that takes immediate effect. Built on native input[type=checkbox] with role='switch'. Visually a sliding toggle. Use for on/off settings where the change applies immediately (not for form fields that are submitted later — use Checkbox for those).

onChange.description: Standard React change handler. For Switch, this typically triggers an immediate side effect (save setting, toggle feature).

requirements: Switch has role='switch' (not 'checkbox') — this signals to screen readers it's an immediate-effect toggle
Must have an accessible label via FormField.Label or aria-label
aria-checked reflects on/off state
Focus ring must be visible

do: Use Switch for settings that take effect immediately (dark mode, notifications on/off, feature flags)
Wrap in FormField for label and layout
Pair the Switch with a label describing the setting in its 'on' meaning ('Email notifications', not 'Toggle notifications')
dont: Don't use Switch for form fields submitted later — use Checkbox
Don't use Switch for choices that aren't binary on/off
Don't use Switch when the change needs confirmation — switches imply immediate, reversible effect

antiPatterns.why: Accepting terms is a form input submitted on form submission, not an immediate-effect setting. Switch implies the change takes effect the instant it's toggled. Use Checkbox for form fields.

use_instead_when.when: the value is part of a form submitted later, or the choice isn't an immediate-effect setting

toast.name: Toast
toast.description: Transient notification that appears in response to an event, auto-dismisses after a timeout, and stacks with other toasts. Unlike every other component in this system, Toast is imperative: you don't render it, you trigger it via the useToast hook. The ToastProvider must wrap the app; useToast returns trigger functions; the visual Toast is rendered by the provider and rarely touched directly. Use for confirmations of background actions (save succeeded, item deleted, upload failed). Not for content that requires acknowledgment — use Dialog for that.

architecture.summary: Toast is three coordinated pieces, not one component.

pieces.name: ToastProvider
pieces.role: Wraps the app (typically at the root). Owns the toast queue state, renders the visual stack in a fixed-position container, manages timers. Configured once with position and default duration.

pieces.name: useToast
pieces.role: Hook consumed anywhere inside the provider. Returns an object with trigger methods (toast.success, toast.error, toast.info, toast.warning, toast.show, and toast.dismiss). This is the API consumers actually use.

pieces.name: Toast (visual)
pieces.role: The individual notification element. Rendered by ToastProvider from queue state. Consumers do not render this directly. Documented here for completeness and styling reference.

position.description: Where the toast stack appears. App-wide setting. bottom-right is the most common convention; top-center is good for high-priority apps where toasts shouldn't be missed.

defaultDuration.description: Default auto-dismiss timeout in milliseconds. Individual toasts can override. Set to 0 for no auto-dismiss (toast persists until manually dismissed). Range guidance: 4000-6000ms for normal messages, longer for messages with actions, 0 for errors the user must see.

max.description: Maximum simultaneous visible toasts. When exceeded, the oldest toast is dismissed to make room. Prevents toast spam from overwhelming the screen.

children.description: The app. ToastProvider wraps everything so useToast is available throughout.

message.description: The toast text. Keep it short — one line ideally. Toasts are glanceable, not readable.

duration.description: Override the provider's defaultDuration for this toast. 0 = no auto-dismiss.

tone.description: Visual tone. Usually set implicitly by which trigger method was called (toast.success sets success). Only needed when using toast.show directly.

action.description: Optional single action button on the toast.

tone.description: Visual tone. Maps to the same palette vocabulary as Badge. Set by the trigger method, not by consumers directly.

onDismiss.description: Called when the toast is dismissed (timeout, manual close, or queue overflow). Wired internally by ToastProvider.

methods.description: Generic toast. tone defaults to neutral unless set in options.
Success toast (tone: success). For confirmations of completed actions.
Danger toast (tone: danger). For failures. Consider duration: 0 so the user doesn't miss it.
Dismiss a specific toast by id, or all toasts if no id given. Each trigger method returns the toast's id for this purpose.

behavior.queue: Toasts stack in trigger order. Newest appears nearest the triggering edge (e.g. bottom for bottom-right). When max is exceeded, the oldest is dismissed.
behavior.timing: Each toast auto-dismisses after its duration (or the provider default). duration:0 disables auto-dismiss.
behavior.pauseOnHover: Hovering any toast pauses the auto-dismiss timer for that toast. Leaving resumes it from where it paused (not a full reset). This prevents toasts vanishing mid-read.
behavior.enterExit: Toasts animate in (slide + fade from the triggering edge) and out (fade + slight slide). Exit animation completes before the toast is removed from the DOM and the stack reflows.
behavior.stacking: Toasts are gap-separated. When one dismisses, the rest reflow to close the gap with a transition.

requirements: The toast stack container has aria-live='polite' so screen readers announce toasts as they appear without interrupting the user
Danger/error toasts may use aria-live='assertive' since failures are higher-priority — consider this per-app
Each toast has role='status'
The dismiss button has an accessible label ('Dismiss notification')

If a toast has an action, the action button must be reachable by keyboard — but note that toasts auto-dismiss, so keyboard users may not have time. For critical actions, use a Dialog instead of a Toast action.
pauseOnHover should also pause on focus-within, so keyboard users tabbing to a toast action get the same reprieve as mouse users
Never put essential information ONLY in a toast — they're transient and easily missed. Toasts confirm, they don't inform-critically.

composition.providerPlacement: ToastProvider wraps the app once, at or near the root. Do not nest ToastProviders.
composition.hookUsage: useToast can be called from any component inside the provider.
forbiddenChildren: any — Toast content is the message string and optional action, not arbitrary children

do: Wrap the app in ToastProvider once, near the root
Use the semantic trigger methods (toast.success, toast.error) rather than toast.show with a tone option — clearer intent
Keep messages short and glanceable — one line
Use toast.error with duration: 0 for failures the user must acknowledge
Use a toast action sparingly — 'Undo' on a delete is the canonical good case
Use toasts to confirm background actions: save, delete, upload, copy
dont: Don't put essential information only in a toast — they vanish and are easily missed
Don't use a toast when the user must make a decision — that's a Dialog
Don't stack many toasts — the max default of 3 is deliberate; if you're triggering more, reconsider
Don't put long messages in toasts — if it doesn't fit one line, it's not a toast
Don't use a toast action for anything destructive or irreversible — the toast may dismiss before the user clicks
Don't render the Toast component directly — trigger via useToast

examples.name: Provider setup
Success confirmation
Error that persists
examples.context: Failure the user must see — duration 0 means no auto-dismiss

examples.name: Toast with undo action
examples.context: The canonical good use of a toast action

antiPatterns.bad: toast.show('Are you sure you want to delete this? Click undo to cancel.')
antiPatterns.why: A toast asking for a decision is the wrong component. Toasts are transient and auto-dismiss — the user may never see it, or it may vanish before they act. Decisions need a Dialog.

antiPatterns.bad: toast.success('Your report for Q3 2024 has been generated and is now available in the Reports section under the Analytics tab. It includes data from all regions.')
antiPatterns.why: Toasts are glanceable, not readable. Long messages don't fit the format and the toast will likely dismiss before a long message can be read. If there's more to say, the toast confirms and a persistent UI element holds the detail.
Triggering many toasts at once overwhelms the screen and hits the max limit, causing toasts to dismiss each other. Aggregate into one toast.
Multiple ToastProviders create multiple independent queues and stacks — toasts from different parts of the app appear in different places with different state. One provider at the root.

use_instead_when.when: the user must make a decision or acknowledge something before continuing
the status is persistent and tied to an element, not a transient event
the message is persistent and inline in the page, not transient — note: Alert not yet built
